statement: Iceland is largely self-sufficient in lamb (over 90% domestic production), dairy products (near 100%), and eggs (near 100%). For other food categories, self-sufficiency is low: approximately 0% for grain and cereals, under 5% for pork, approximately 15–20% for poultry, and under 10% for fruits and vegetables (excluding greenhouse-grown tomatoes and cucumbers where domestic production is significant). Overall caloric self-sufficiency is estimated at approximately 45–50%.
source_name: Matvælastofnun (MAST) — Icelandic Food and Veterinary Authority; Bændasamtök Íslands
caveats: Self-sufficiency figures vary by measure (caloric, weight, value). Iceland's high self-sufficiency in lamb and dairy reflects tariff protection rather than comparative advantage. Climate limits grain production entirely. Food security concerns are sometimes cited against EU membership (loss of tariff protection), though EU membership would diversify supply chains.

related_entries.statement: Iceland's agricultural sector contributed approximately 1.0–1.2% of GDP in 2024 and employed around 3,500 people (roughly 1.7% of the labour force). The sector is dominated by sheep farming (approximately 450,000 sheep), dairy production (approximately 30,000 cattle), and greenhouse horticulture (using geothermal energy). Iceland has approximately 2,300 farms, with average farm size significantly smaller than the EU average.

Specific food price comparisons (2024 Eurostat data, EU-27 = 100): Iceland dairy products index approximately 165–180, meat products approximately 155–170, bread and cereals approximately 140–155, fruits and vegetables approximately 135–150. The highest premiums are on products that compete directly with domestic production (dairy, lamb) where tariff protection is strongest.
